title: What TLS Provides
description:
## Securing the Connection

When a browser connects to `https://` site, a **TLS** handshake takes place before any HTTP data is exchanged: the server presents a **certificate**, the parties agree on keys, and the session is encrypted.

Which statement best describes what TLS actually provides?

options.text: **Confidentiality** (encryption of data **in transit**), **server authentication** via the server's certificate, and **integrity** so tampering is detected.
It encrypts your data **at rest** on the server's disk, so stored files are safe even if the disk is stolen.
It guarantees the web application has no bugs or vulnerabilities.
It speeds up the connection by compressing all responses.

explanation: **TLS** secures data **in transit** on the wire. It provides three things: **confidentiality** — the session is encrypted so eavesdroppers see only ciphertext; **authentication** — the server proves its identity with a certificate signed by a trusted Certificate Authority, so the client knows it's talking to the real server (not an impostor); and **integrity** — message authentication detects any tampering with the data en route. During the handshake the parties verify the certificate and negotiate session keys. TLS does **not** encrypt data **at rest** on disk (b) — that's a separate concern (disk/database encryption), it cannot make application code bug-free (c), and modern TLS does not compress responses to speed them up (d).

hints.title: In transit vs at rest
hints.body: TLS protects bytes while they travel over the network. Protecting bytes sitting on a disk is a different mechanism entirely.
